text.title: Six-Step Voice UI Design Process
text.summary: The ordered design process from use-case selection to user testing, with concrete outputs at each step.

rule.statement: Identify tasks where hands-free or eyes-free operation provides measurable value before touching dialogue design.
rule.rationale: Designing voice for unsuitable tasks wastes NLU budget and produces frustrated users.

rule.statement: Write natural-language happy-path and error dialogues before defining intents, slots, or NLU configs.
rule.rationale: Dialogue-first design catches unnatural phrasing and missing recovery paths before any code is written.

example.code:
User: "Set a timer for 15 minutes" System: "Timer set for 15 minutes, starting now."
User: "Add milk to my shopping list" System: "I've added milk to your shopping list."

rule.statement: Each intent must have at least 5 sample utterances covering natural phrasing variations.
rule.rationale: NLU models underfit without phrasing diversity; users say the same thing dozens of different ways.

example.code:
Intent: ControlLight Slots: - room (required): "living room", "kitchen" - action (required): "on", "off", "dim"
Sample utterances: - "Turn off the living room lights" - "Lights off in the living room" - "Living room lights off" - "Switch off the living room lights" - "Kill the lights in the living room"

rule.statement: Write initial prompts as single questions; keep error prompts to one sentence plus one example.
rule.rationale: Long prompts force users to remember too much audio; concision reduces cognitive load and re-prompting.

example.code: Initial: "What would you like to order?" Error: "I didn't catch that. Try saying a size like 'medium' or 'large'." Confirm: "That's a large pepperoni for pickup at noon. Should I place the order?"
"Hello! Welcome to Pizza Palace, the best pizza in town! We have so many great options! What can I get for you today?"
example.note: Verbose initial prompts increase time-to-first-response and are perceived as robotic.

section.title: Step 5: Design a Three-Tier Error Ladder

rule.statement: Every intent must have a three-tier error strategy: rephrase → examples → fallback (agent or visual).
rule.rationale: Single-tier error handling ("Please try again") is the primary cause of user abandonment in voice.

list.item: 1st error: rephrase the original prompt more concisely.
2nd error: rephrase with concrete examples of valid input.
3rd error: offer a human agent transfer or visual alternative.

rule.statement: Run Wizard-of-Oz testing (human plays the system) before any NLU training begins.
rule.rationale: WoZ reveals what users actually say — which is often different from every utterance the team wrote.
